coredata.dc:title: TiO2, SnO2 and ZnO catalysts supported on mesoporous SBA-15 versus unsupported nanopowders in photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Calzada, Lina A.
Castellanos, Rafael
García, Luis A.
Klimova, Tatiana E.
coredata.dc:description: Semiconductor metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO or SnO2) deposited on a highly ordered mesoporous silica material (called SBA-15 in the literature) were compared for the first time as photocatalysts in the elimination of cationic methylene blue (MB) dye from neutral aqueous solutions by adsorption and/or photodegradation under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. Prepared catalysts were characterized by N2 physisorption, X-ray diffraction, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, ammonia temperature-programmed desorption, isoelectric point determination, scanning and transmission electron microscopy. Characterization results showed that the mesoporous structure of the SBA-15 was preserved in the catalysts. However, the dispersion of metal oxides in the catalysts was very different. Zinc oxide was well-dispersed in the ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst, whereas TiO2 and SnO2 crystalline phases were detected in the TiO2/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 samples, being SnO2 oxide the worst-dispersed. All SBA-15-supported catalysts showed high capacity for MB adsorption, especially the ZnO/SBA-15 one, and had a significantly higher (2–5 times) photocatalytic activity than the corresponding commercial TiO2, ZnO and SnO2 nanopowders. The TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 catalysts resulted in a higher degree of mineralization of MB dye (63%) than titania Degussa P25 (39%) in the photocatalytic degradation of MB after the adsorption step. This was attributed to the higher specific surface area of the SBA-15-supported catalysts, their higher adsorption capacity and better dispersion of the supported metal oxides in comparison with titania P25. In addition, deposition of ZnO on the SBA-15 surface prevented the photocorrosion of ZnO that is an important advantage compared to pure ZnO nanoparticles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM-EDX mapping of Ti, Zn and Sn in the prepared TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (A) and pore size distributions (B) of SBA-15 support and TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts. Results for titania Degussa P25 are also shown for comparison purposes.
Small-angle XRD patterns of SBA-15 support and TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts.

Powder XRD patterns of the SBA-15, TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 samples (A) and of the commercial nanopowders (B). Signals were assigned to TiO2 anatase crystalline phase (•, JCPDS No. 21–1272), TiO2 rutile crystalline phase (o, JCPDS No. 88–1172), ZnO zincite crystalline phase (Δ, JCPDS No. 89–7102) and SnO2 cassiterite crystalline phase (*, JCPDS No. 41–1445).
TEM images of: (a, b) SBA-15 support, (c, d) TiO2/SBA-15, (e, f) ZnO/SBA-15 and (g, h) SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts.

(A) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (B) modified Kubelka-Munk of synthesized photocatalysts and commercial titania Degussa P25 (n = 2 for TiO2/SBA-15 and P25 and n = 1/2 for ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15).
Ammonia TPD profiles of the prepared catalysts and titania Degussa P25.

Photocatalytic degradation of MB on TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15, SnO2/SBA-15 and TiO2 P25: (A) 120 min in dark followed by 120 min under UV irradiation (λ = 254 nm), (B) 120 min under UV irradiation (λ = 254 nm) starts from the beginning of the experiments.

Absorption spectra of methylene blue solutions obtained at different reaction times in the first set of experiments (120 min in dark followed by 120 min under UV irradiation, 20 ppm initial MB concentration) with different photocatalysts. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Images of the initial MB solution (a), MB solution after photocatalytic experiment with titania Degussa P25 catalyst (2 h under UV irradiation upon stirring) and then 15 h without stirring and (c) MB solution after photocatalytic experiment with TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst (2 h under UV irradiation upon stirring) followed by 2 min without stirring. In the case (b), no catalyst's separation from the reaction solution was observed, whereas in the case (c), the catalyst quickly separated from the solution.

Results from the reusability of the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst in the photodegradation of methylene blue (experimental conditions: 120 min in dark followed by 120 min under UV irradiation, 20 ppm initial MB concentration). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: TiO2, SnO2 and ZnO catalysts supported on mesoporous SBA-15 versus unsupported nanopowders in photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratorio de Nanocatálisis, Departamento de Ingeniería Química, Facultad de Química, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Cd. Universitaria, Coyoacán, CDMX, 04510, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratorio de Nanocatalisis, Departamento de Ingenieria Quimica, Facultad de Quimica, Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), Cd. Universitaria, Coyoacan, CDMX, 04510, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Semiconductor metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO or SnO2) deposited on a highly ordered mesoporous silica material (called SBA-15 in the literature) were compared for the first time as photocatalysts in the elimination of cationic methylene blue (MB) dye from neutral aqueous solutions by adsorption and/or photodegradation under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. Prepared catalysts were characterized by N2 physisorption, X-ray diffraction, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, ammonia temperature-programmed desorption, isoelectric point determination, scanning and transmission electron microscopy. Characterization results showed that the mesoporous structure of the SBA-15 was preserved in the catalysts. However, the dispersion of metal oxides in the catalysts was very different. Zinc oxide was well-dispersed in the ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst, whereas TiO2 and SnO2 crystalline phases were detected in the TiO2/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 samples, being SnO2 oxide the worst-dispersed. All SBA-15-supported catalysts showed high capacity for MB adsorption, especially the ZnO/SBA-15 one, and had a significantly higher (2–5 times) photocatalytic activity than the corresponding commercial TiO2, ZnO and SnO2 nanopowders. The TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 catalysts resulted in a higher degree of mineralization of MB dye (63%) than titania Degussa P25 (39%) in the photocatalytic degradation of MB after the adsorption step. This was attributed to the higher specific surface area of the SBA-15-supported catalysts, their higher adsorption capacity and better dispersion of the supported metal oxides in comparison with titania P25. In addition, deposition of ZnO on the SBA-15 surface prevented the photocorrosion of ZnO that is an important advantage compared to pure ZnO nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic dyes, discharged from different industrial effluents, cause environmental pollution and generate adverse effects in the environment. Some highly stable organic dyes are not affected by temperature or light. Therefore, their elimination from water effluents has become an important topic of study. Up to now, many methods have been tested for the removal/degradation of organic pollutants, for example, photocatalytic degradation, adsorption, ozonation, Fenton, advanced oxidation, biodegradation, etc. [1–4]. Among the above methods, photocatalytic degradation is the most widely used because of a number of advantages: use of oxygen as an oxidant, low temperature and complete mineralization [5,6]. Generally, conventional non-porous semiconductor metal oxides, such as TiO2, SnO2, ZnO, etc., are used as photocatalysts, because when they are irradiated with light of an energy equal to or higher than their band gap energy, the generation of charge carriers, denominated electron-hole pairs, is carried out. The electron-hole pairs are responsible for the production of radicals, which are involved in the redox reactions that take place on the surface of the semiconductor, achieving the mineralization of the organic pollutants [1–3,7–11]. It is worth to mention that recently, a large progress was made in the application of semiconductor nanoparticles (quantum dots, QDs), especially zinc sulfide QDs with limited dimensions of 1–10 nm and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), in the photocatalytic degradation of different cationic and anionic dyes [4–6,12,13]. These nanomaterials are especially attractive because of their high photocatalytic performance at UV or even visible light irradiation (GQDs). However, their possible application at a large industrial scale seems to be challenging, because a very small particle size makes difficult or almost impossible the separation and recovery of the nanophotocatalysts from the photoreaction systems. To improve the separation of the photocatalysts, semiconductor materials can be deposited on high surface area and porosity supports [14].

Recently, a highly ordered mesoporous silica material, called SBA-15 in the literature, has been successfully employed as a support for titania photocatalysts, increasing their photocatalytic performance in the degradation of organic pollutants. The improvement in their photocatalytic behavior has been attributed to a decrease in the size of the supported TiO2 crystallites, which increases the surface area, resulting in a higher amount of active sites [15–21]. Moreover, the relatively large (6–8 nm diameter) open channels of the mesoporous SBA-15 offer an advantage in terms of diffusion of the reactants towards the surface of the semiconductor particles and of the obtained products to the outside of the catalyst [22–24].

On the other hand, ZnO and SnO2 supported on SBA-15 materials have been less studied than TiO2/SBA-15 catalysts. Mihai et al. [23] reported that ZnO deposited on SBA-15 showed higher activity in methylene blue (MB) dye degradation than the ZnO/MCM-41 material. The better photocatalytic response was attributed to the smaller size of the ZnO particles and the larger pore size of the SBA-15 support that avoided the blockage of pores. Maučec et al. [25] compared the behavior of TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles deposited on ordered and disordered mesoporous silicas (SBA-15 and KIL2, respectively) in the degradation of anionic Reactive Blue 19 (RB19) dye at neutral pH. Disordered ZnO/silica with a Zn:Si molar ratio 1:1 showed the highest RB19 adsorption and the fastest dye degradation. A considerable difference between ZnO and TiO2 composites was observed in RB19 adsorption, which was attributed to the positive charge of the ZnO surface, creating strong electrostatic attraction between ZnO and negatively charged anionic dye molecules. Meanwhile, the negative charge of TiO2 at neutral pH results in repulsion between its surface and RB19. Vo et al. [26] observed that N-dopped ZnO/SBA-15 materials showed better MB degradation under visible light than the unsupported N-dopped ZnO catalyst. SnO2/SBA-15 has been prepared and evaluated in the degradation of MB [27] and rhodamine B [28] dyes. The latter studies showed that the good dispersion of the tin(IV) oxide inside the mesoporous silica caused the better photocatalytic response.

In the present work, a comparison study of the photocatalytic behavior of Ti(IV), Sn(IV) and Zn(II) oxides supported on SBA-15 and commercially available TiO2, ZnO and SnO2 nanopowders and TiO2 Degussa P25 was performed in the removal of cationic methylene blue dye by adsorption and degradation under UV irradiation. To the best of our knowledge, no similar study was reported previously for cationic dye elimination from wastewater. Our interest was in searching for the advantages that the deposition of the above-mentioned semiconductor oxides on the SBA-15 matrix had on their photocatalytic activity and the possibility of quick separation from the treated water required for the catalyst's reusing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SBA-15 support and catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The SBA-15 support was synthesized according to the Zhao et al. procedure [29]. Triblock copolymer Pluronic P123 (Mav = 5800, EO20PO70EO20) was employed as a template and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98% Aldrich) as a silica source. The nominal molar ratio of the chemicals used in the synthesis was 1TEOS:0.017P123:5.95HCl:171H2O. The hydrothermal treatment was performed first upon stirring at 35 °C (20 h) and then without stirring at 80 °C (48 h). The obtained solid was washed until neutral pH with deionized water, air-dried at room temperature and calcined in static air at 550 °C for 6 h.

TiO2-, SnO2- and ZnO-containing SBA-15 catalysts were prepared by the incipient wetness impregnation technique using Ti(IV) isopropoxide solution in n-propanol (1.68 M, Ti(i-Pro)4, 97%, Aldrich), and aqueous solutions of tin(IV) chloride (1.10 M, SnCl4·5H2O, 98%, Aldrich) and zinc(II) nitrate (2.05 M, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 98% Aldrich), respectively. Previous to the catalyst's preparation, the impregnation volume of the SBA-15 support (the volume of the liquid required to fill the pores) was determined with the solvents. It was found to be 3.2 mL/g for n-propanol and 2.6 mL/g for water. Then the SBA-15 support (1 g) was impregnated with the corresponding volume of each precursor solution. The catalysts were dried in air at room temperature for 24 h, then at 100 °C for 12 h and calcined in static air at 500 °C for 4 h to ensure the complete transformation of the precursors used, to the corresponding metal oxides. The concentrations of the precursor solutions were calculated to obtain 30 wt % nominal loadings of TiO2, SnO2 and ZnO in the catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SBA-15 support and catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared SBA-15 support and metal oxide-containing catalysts were characterized by N2 physisorption, powder and small-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD and SA-XRD), UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS), isoelectric point (IEP), ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (ammonia TPD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM-EDX) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM).

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured with a Micromeritics 3-FLEX automatic analyzer at liquid N2 temperature (−197.5 °C). Prior to the experiments, the samples were degassed (p < 10−1 Pa) at 270 °C for 6 h. Specific surface areas (SBET) were calculated by the BET method. The total pore volumes (Vp) were determined by nitrogen adsorption at a relative pressure of 0.98. Pore size distributions were obtained from the adsorption and desorption isotherms by the BJH method. The mesopore diameters (Dads and Ddes) correspond to the maxima of pore size distribution curves. The micropore area (Sμ) was estimated using the correlation of t-Harkins & Jura (t-plot method).

XRD patterns were recorded in the 3°–80° (2θ) range on a Siemens D5000 diffractometer, using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) and a goniometer speed of 1° (2θ)/min. Small-angle XRD (SA-XRD, 2θ = 0.5–10°) was performed on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using small divergence and scattering slits of 0.05°. The a0 unit-cell parameter of SBA-15-type materials was estimated from the position of the (100) diffraction line as a0 = 2∕√3 × d 100. Pore wall thickness, δ, was assessed by subtracting Dp from the a0 unit-cell parameter, which corresponds to the distance between centers of adjacent mesopores.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the catalysts were recorded in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm at room temperature using a Varian Cary 100 Conc spectrophotometer equipped with a diffuse reflectance attachment, employing polytetrafluoroethylene as a reference.

Zeta potential was measured by a Zeta-meter 3.0 + equipment using an electrophoretic cell in the pH range of 2–12 adjusted by the addition of 0.1 M KOH or 0.1 M HCl. The pH value of each suspension was determined on a digital Metrohm pH meter. The isoelectric point (IEP) of each sample was determined as the pH value corresponding to the zeta potential value equal to 0 mV.

The temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (ammonia TPD) was performed in a Micromeritics AutoChem II 2920 automatic analyzer equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Prior to the measurements, the samples (50 mg) were pretreated in situ at 500 °C for 30 min in a helium flow to remove water and other contaminants. After the samples were cooled to 120 °C, they were contacted with a NH3/He mixture (10:90 mol/mol, 20 mL/min flow) for 30 min and then purged with pure He (20 mL/min flow) for 30 min. The desorption step was performed in a He stream (50 mL/min) from 120 °C to 500 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min, keeping the final temperature constant until the trace reached the baseline.

The chemical composition of the catalysts was determined by SEM-EDX using a JEOL 5900 LV microscope with OXFORD ISIS equipment. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) studies were performed using a JEOL 2010 microscope (resolving power 1.9 Å at 200 kV).

The catalysts were evaluated in the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) in a batch cylindrical glass reactor equipped with an external jacket connected to a recirculation bath maintaining the temperature at 20 °C. Typically, 0.02 g of catalyst were added to 150 mL of aqueous MB solution of 20 ppm concentration. Commercial titania Degussa P25 (TiO2 P25) and unsupported TiO2, ZnO and SnO2 nanopowders (Aldrich) were also evaluated for comparison purposes. Two types of experiments were performed: in the first one, the suspension was stirred for 120 min in the dark to ensure the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, after which irradiation with a UV pen ray Hg lamp (λ = 254 nm, Io = 4400 μW cm−2) settled into a quartz tube holder at the center of the reactor, was turned on for another 120 min. In the second set of experiments, the UV irradiation was turned on since the beginning of the experiment and maintained for 120 min. To follow MB removal, aliquots of the reaction mixture were withdrawn at regular time intervals, filtered with a nitrocellulose membrane of 0.22 μm and analyzed in a UV–visible Varian Cary 100 Conc spectrophotometer. The decrease in the intensity of the most prominent absorption band of the MB spectra, typically at 663 nm, was used to follow the degradation of the MB solution.

Total organic carbon (TOC) concentration was determined by a Shimadzu TOC analyzer TOC-V CSN with an ASI-V sampler. To follow the mineralization of the MB, 20 mL aliquots were withdrawn at different reaction times, filtered with a nitrocellulose membrane of 0.22 μm and analyzed in Shimadzu TOC analyzer. The total carbon (TC) concentration was obtained after the catalytic oxidation of the sample by combustion at 680 °C with a platinum catalyst. The carbon dioxide generated in this process was detected in an infrared gas analyzer (NDIR). For the inorganic carbon (IC) concentration, the sample was acidified with HCl and the formed carbon dioxide was determined by the NDIR detector. Then, the TOC concentration was calculated by subtracting the IC concentration from the obtained TC concentration (TOC = TC – IC), which was performed automatically by the equipment.

The structural and textural properties of the SBA-15 support and the prepared photocatalysts are summarized in Table 1. The SBA-15 showed high specific surface area and pore volume (730 m2/g and 1.07 cm3/g, respectively). After the incorporation of Ti(IV), Zn(II) and Sn(IV) oxides, the specific textural characteristics (SBET, Sμ and VP) decreased, which was attributed to an increase in the materials’ density due to the deposition of 30 wt % of nonporous metal oxides. Nevertheless, the textural characteristics of the prepared catalysts were much higher than those of the commercially available TiO2, ZnO and SnO2 nanopowders (Table 1).

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms are exhibited in Fig. 2 A. All of them are characteristic to SBA-15-type mesoporous materials, corresponding to type IV according to the IUPAC classification. The hysteresis loop of the SBA-15 support can be clearly ascribed to a H1 type characteristic of the materials with nearly cylindrical pore channels. No significant changes in the shape of the hysteresis were observed after the deposition of ZnO and SnO2 metal oxides, which indicates that the mesoporous structure of the starting SBA-15 was preserved in the ZnO- and SnO2-containing catalysts. These results are consistent with the pore size distributions, presented in Fig. 2B, where narrow monomodal pore distributions can be observed for the SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 samples. Regarding the TiO2-containing catalyst, it can be noticed in Fig. 2A that the capillary condensation/evaporation steps of this sample (hysteresis loop) are located at lower relative pressures than the other samples. This result evidences that the diameter of the cylindrical pores of the SBA-15 support decreased after the deposition of titania. According to Table 1 and Fig. 2B, the pore size of the SBA-15 support determined from the adsorption branch of the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm (Dads) decreased from 80 Å to 65 Å in the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst, while it was equal to 76 Å in the ZnO- and SnO2-containing samples. To explain this result, it can be assumed that a part of the semiconductor oxide species were deposited inside the mesopore channels of the SBA-15 support and that the inner titania species are more agglomerated than the ZnO or SnO2 ones. This seems to be reasonable considering a much higher hydrolysis rate of the Ti(IV) isopropoxide used as a precursor for the preparation of the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst in comparison with other water-soluble tin(IV) chloride and zinc(II) nitrate precursors. On the other side, the above supposition does not exclude the possible coexistence of the inner-deposited metal oxide species with those agglomerated in the SBA-15 pore mouths or on the external surface of the SBA-15 support. In the particular case of the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst, we also observed a decrease in the pore diameters determined from the desorption branches of the isotherms (Ddes, Table 1) from 63 Å in the starting SBA-15 support to 42 Å for the titania-containing sample. Therefore, the agglomeration of titania in the pore mouths of the SBA-15 support can also be expected. For the other two catalysts, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15, only a slight decrease (∼1–3 Ǻ) in the desorption pore diameter was detected after deposition of zinc and tin oxides.

The small-angle XRD patterns of the SBA-15 support and prepared catalysts (Fig. 3 ) exhibited three peaks located at approximately 0.87, 1.51 and 1.77° (2θ). These signals are associated with the characteristic p6mm hexagonal symmetry of cylindrical pores, typical of well-ordered SBA-15 material, and can be ascribed to the (100), (110) and (200) reflections [29]. Almost no changes were detected in the position of the above reflections after the deposition of the metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO and SnO2) on the SBA-15 support. However, the intensity of the signals decreased in the following order ZnO/SBA-15 > SnO2/SBA-15 > TiO2/SBA-15 indicating that some loss of the long-range pore arrangement took place in the same order. In general, such a decrease in the intensity of the characteristic SBA-15 reflections is observed when some of the support's pores are fully or partially blocked by the deposited species leading to a less regular hexagonal arrangement of the pores. Based on this, the low intensity of the signals obtained for the TiO2/SBA-15 sample is a strong indication of the obstruction of the SBA-15 pore mouths by the agglomerated titanium oxide, which is in line with the N2 physisorption results described above. A similar effect, namely a plugging of the SBA-15 pores by the agglomerated metal oxide species, can also be assumed for the SnO2/SBA-15 catalyst, but in a smaller extent than for the TiO2/SBA-15 one. The small-angle XRD results were also used to determine the hexagonal unit cell parameter (a0) and the pore wall thickness (δ) values reported in Table 1. It was observed that the a0 value of all materials was 115–117 Å, meanwhile the pore wall thickness increased from 35 Å in the SBA-15 support to 41–50 Å in the metal oxide-containing catalysts, again being the largest wall thickness of the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst.

Fig. 5 shows the TEM images of the SBA-15 support and the prepared supported catalysts. It can be observed that the pristine SBA-15 exhibits the parallel channels, as well as the regular hexagonal array of mesopores characteristic of SBA-15 materials (Fig. 5a,b). The images of the TiO2-, ZnO- and SnO2-containing catalysts (Fig. 5c–h) confirm that the characteristic mesopore structure of the SBA-15 support was preserved in the prepared catalysts, which is in line with the small-angle XRD results. In addition, for the ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst, it was observed that only small particles of ZnO were homogeneously dispersed inside the pore channels of the SBA-15 support (Fig. 5e,f). No formation of large ZnO particles was detected on the external surface of the SBA-15 support, in accordance with the XRD characterization results. The TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst exhibited the presence of titanium oxide nanoparticles of different sizes inside the SBA-15 mesopores (Fig. 5c) and some larger particles (∼8–12 nm) blocking pore entrances of the SBA-15 support (Fig. 5d). This result is in line with the XRD characterization of this catalyst, where the presence of TiO2 anatase crystalline phase with a mean crystallite size of 8.8 nm was detected. In contrast, the image of the SnO2/SBA-15 catalyst showed the presence of a mixture of tin dioxide particles with different degrees of agglomeration, small ones inside the SBA-15 pores and some large particles (∼20–70 nm) on the external surface of the SBA-15 support (Fig. 5g,h). In this case, the average size of SnO2 crystallites estimated from the XRD pattern was about 10.5 nm, which is significantly smaller than the size of the metal oxide particles observed on the external surface of the SBA-15 support by TEM. However, a more detailed inspection of the image shown in Fig. 5h reveals the irregular shape of the large metal oxide particles located on the external surface of the SBA-15 support, which allows assuming that such large particles consist of the agglomeration of smaller SnO2 crystallites. Therefore, according to the TEM results, the dispersion of metal oxides increases in the following order: SnO2/SBA-15 < TiO2/SBA-15 < ZnO/SBA-15.

In order to obtain more information related to the electronic properties of the synthesized catalysts, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded (Fig. 6 A). Commercial titania Degussa P25 sample was also included for comparison. It can be observed that all tested materials showed absorption in a 200–400 nm wavelength region pointing to their semiconductor properties. The position of the absorption edge of the prepared catalysts was blue shifted in comparison with that of the commercial TiO2 P25, suggesting higher values of band gap energy (Eg) in the catalysts supported on SBA-15. The Eg values of the samples were determined employing the modified Kubelka-Munk (K-M) function (Fig. 6B). For TiO2 P25 and TiO2/SBA-15, an indirect allowed transition was considered (n = 2) [30–32], while for the ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 samples, a direct allowed transition was used (n = 1/2) [30]. The Eg values, reported in Table 2, were obtained from the extrapolation of the slopes of the modified K-M functions versus energy to zero absorption (Fig. 6B). The results for the commercial anatase Degussa P25 showed a value (3.21 eV) close to the value reported for titania anatase (3.2 eV) [31]. On the other hand, the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst revealed a slight increase in the Eg (3.30 eV), which could be attributed to the smaller size of TiO2 particles in the prepared catalyst supported on SBA-15 [15,21]. The ZnO/SBA-15 sample also showed a higher Eg value (4.01 eV) than the corresponding bulk ZnO (3.30 eV) that could also be attributed to the quantum size effect, caused by the very small size of the supported ZnO particles on the ZnO/SBA-15 sample [23,33], as it was confirmed by the XRD and TEM characterizations. In line with the above results, the SnO2/SBA-15 sample showed only a slightly higher Eg value (4.06 eV) than pure bulk SnO2 (4.02 eV). This small difference in the Eg values of the supported and unsupported SnO2 could be attributed to a low dispersion of the tin oxide on the SBA-15 surface and the formation of the large SnO2 particles detected on the external surface of the catalyst by XRD and TEM [34].

The isoelectric point (IEP) values obtained for the prepared catalysts and unsupported commercial nanopowders are shown in Table 2. The IEP of unmodified SBA-15 was observed at 2.3, which is in good agreement with previously published values (2.1–2.5) [35,36]. IEPs of other pure materials were 4.5 for SnO2, 6.0 for TiO2 anatase nanopowder, 6.9 for titania Degussa P25 and 9.4 for ZnO nanopowder. The prepared SBA-15-supported catalysts showed IEP values intermediate between those of the SBA-15 silica and the corresponding unsupported metal oxides. Thus, the IEP values obtained for the TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts were 5.6, 5.8 and 2.4, respectively. The above results indicate that at neutral pH value (pH = 7) of the aqueous solution of MB used in the photocatalytic experiments, the majority of the samples will be negatively charged, being only the commercial ZnO nanopowder strongly positively charged. Previously, it was shown that the surface charge of the materials used as photocatalysts can play an important role in their interaction with cationic or anionic dyes, which are positively or negatively charged in aqueous solutions, leading to their electrostatic attraction or repulsion from the catalyst's surface. The above can affect the efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes [25].

Finally, the acidic properties of the prepared TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts were also characterized by temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (Fig. 7 , Table 2). It was found that all catalysts showed the presence of mainly weak and medium acidic sites with a small proportion of strong ones. The ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst resulted the most acidic among all catalysts supported on SBA-15, followed by the TiO2/SBA-15 sample. It can be observed that if the starting SBA-15 support had only a very small amount of acidic sites (41 μmol NH3 g−1, Table 2), the incorporation of TiO2 and ZnO oxides on its surface resulted in a strong increase in the catalysts’ acidity (8–9 times) that can be ascribed to the contribution of the deposited TiO2 and ZnO species. According to the above-described results from the XRD and TEM characterization of the prepared MOx/SBA-15 catalysts, the dispersion of the metal oxides in them increases in the following order: SnO2/SBA-15 < TiO2/SBA-15 < ZnO/SBA-15. It can be noted that the total amount of acidic sites in these catalysts follows the same order (Table 2, last column) confirming that new acidic sites are due to the MOx component of the MOx/SBA-15 catalysts.

After reaching the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and turning on the UV lamp, the concentration of MB in the reaction solutions continued decreasing due to its photocatalytic degradation in the presence of the prepared SBA-15-supported catalysts or commercially available titania P25 (Fig. 8A). In order to compare the photocatalytic activity of different catalysts, the apparent first-order reaction rate constants were calculated (k
app , Table 3). It can be noticed that in this set of experiments (Ads + UV), the TiO2/SBA-15 catalyst exhibited the highest k
app value (0.0226 min−1) followed by the ZnO/SBA-15 and TiO2 P25 catalysts, which showed k
app values of 0.0194 min−1 and 0.0159 min−1, respectively. The SnO2/SBA-15 sample had the lowest k
app (0.0137 min−1) among all SBA-15-supported catalysts, which can be attributed to a low dispersion of the SnO2 active species according to the results from the XRD and TEM characterizations. The above constants were calculated for the experiments, in which the initial concentration of the MB dye was different at the moment of starting the UV irradiation, due to the adsorption of different amounts of MB on the tested catalysts during the dark period.

It is well known that pure unsupported ZnO has some disadvantages compared to TiO2, being the photocorrosion of ZnO in aqueous solutions under UV irradiation the most important one [41–43]. In order to inquire into the ZnO photocorrosion in the above set of experiments, we performed the characterization of the spent ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst by SEM-EDX. It was observed that the ZnO loading in the used catalyst (31.9 wt %) was almost equal to that in the freshly-prepared ZnO/SBA-15 sample (31.3 wt %, Table 1). This showed that no ZnO dissolution had taken place upon the photocatalytic experiment. For comparison, a similar experiment was performed with 20 mg of the commercial ZnO nanopowder. In this case, the catalyst was separated from the reaction solution after finishing the photocatalytic test and the amount of the catalyst decreased to 9 mg, indicating that more than 50% of the starting ZnO was lost by dissolution. The above result indicates that the deposition of the ZnO on the SBA-15 surface inhibited its photocorrosion. A similar behavior was also observed recently for well-dispersed ZnO nanoparticles immobilized on the mesoporous silica supports KIL-2 and SBA-15 [25]. In this case, increased resistance of ZnO to photocorrosion was attributed to the interaction between SiO2 and ZnO, resulting in improved stability of the ZnO catalysts under UV irradiation.

A second set of experiments was performed with the aim to compare the photodegradation activity of the catalysts at the same initial concentration of MB in the reaction solution. In this case, the UV irradiation was turned on at the beginning of the reaction (without adsorption in the dark period). The results of the photolysis of the MB solution (without catalyst) were also obtained and shown in Fig. 8B for comparison purposes. It can be seen in this figure, that the initial activities of the commercial TiO2 P25 and the TiO2/SBA-15 were similar. However, after 30 min of reaction, the activity of the titania P25 strongly decreased, while TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 samples continued removing the MB dye until the end of the experiment. Among the prepared SBA-15-supported catalysts, the TiO2/SBA-15 showed the highest k
app value (0.0281 min−1, Table 3), meanwhile the SnO2/SBA-15 showed the lowest photocatalytic performance (0.0156 min−1). Once again, both TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 catalysts showed higher photocatalytic activity than the reference titania Degussa P25. A comparison between the k
app values obtained in the first set of experiments (Ads + UV) and in the second one (UV) (Table 3) showed that the rate constants calculated for the second set of experiments had higher values. This can be attributed to the fact that, when the UV irradiation was started from the beginning of the experiments, adsorption of MB dye and its photodegradation took place at the same time increasing the rate of MB decolorization and the corresponding k
app value, especially for the samples with high adsorption capacity.

In order to study the effect of the MB concentration on the photocatalytic behavior of the prepared TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts, experiments with different initial concentrations of MB (10, 20 and 30 ppm) were performed following the conditions of the first set of experiments (120 min in dark and then 120 min under UV irradiation). The obtained results are shown in Table 4 . It can be observed that the adsorption capacity (qe) of the SBA-15-supported catalysts increased with an increase in the initial MB concentration. This is in line with previous report [40], where it was shown that the adsorption capacities increase with increasing the equilibrium concentration of the dye until the adsorption saturation is achieved. This was attributed to the fact that higher initial dye concentration provides higher driving force, by which the diffusion of dye molecules onto the surface of the adsorbent will be accelerated. The ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst showed maximum adsorption capacity (85 mg/gcat) among all tested catalysts at 30 ppm concentration of MB solution. On the other hand, an increment in the MB adsorption with increasing initial MB concentration is accompanied by a decrease in the percentage of MB decolorization under UV irradiation and the corresponding k
app values (Table 4). Thus, at an initial MB concentration of 10 ppm, 97–98% of MB decolorization was reached, while at 30 ppm concentration, only 56–69% decrease in the MB concentration was obtained at the end of the experiments. This means that a high amount of MB dye adsorbed on the catalyst's surface inhibit its performance as a photocatalyst, probably, due to a decrease in the amount of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The above results illustrate that the SBA-15-supported catalysts synthesized in the present work show a better photocatalytic performance at MB concentrations below 20 ppm.

In the above-mentioned experiments, the course of the reaction was followed by the spectrophotometrical determination of the MB concentration at different reaction times. This method does not represent the real degree of the mineralization of the methylene blue dye, because some colorless organic subproducts can be formed during the reaction in addition to the products of complete mineralization of MB dye (gaseous CO2, water, nitrate, ammonium and sulfate ions). In order to characterize the real degree of mineralization (degradation) of the MB dye with the synthesized catalysts, the total amount of organic carbon (TOC) remaining in the solutions was determined at different reaction times and after the reactions were over. Table 4 shows the percentage of mineralization of the MB dye at the end of the experiments with different initial MB concentrations. In all cases, degree of MB mineralization was lower than the corresponding degree of decolorization, indicating the existence of some colorless organic degradation products. The reference TiO2 Degussa P-25 catalyst showed the lowest mineralization of MB (39%), while all prepared photocatalysts supported on SBA-15 resulted in higher percentage of MB mineralization. Better mineralization results were obtained for 10 and 20 ppm MB concentrations. The percentage of MB mineralization in the experiments performed maintaining constant the initial MB concentration increased in the following order TiO2 P25 < SnO2/SBA-15 < ZnO/SBA-15 ≈ TiO2/SBA-15 (Table 4). It is worth to mention that this order differs from the order of increasing k
app constants shown in the same table: SnO2/SBA-15 < TiO2 P25 < ZnO/SBA-15 < TiO2/SBA-15. Nevertheless, two of the synthesized catalysts (ZnO/SBA-15 and TiO2/SBA-15) showed in both cases (decolorization and mineralization) better results than those obtained with TiO2 P25 reference. The mineralization of MB dye obtained with ZnO and TiO2 oxides supported on SBA-15 silica were similar to those reported by us previously, when hydrogen titanate nanotubes were used as a photocatalyst for MB degradation in similar experimental conditions (20 ppm of MB initial concentration) [39].

To obtain more information on the photocatalytic processes occurring during the photocatalytic degradation of the MB dye, the absorption spectra of MB solutions obtained at different reaction times with TiO2 Degussa P-25, TiO2/SBA-15, ZnO/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts in the first series of experiments (adsorption for 120 min in dark followed by UV irradiation for another 120 min) are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be observed that in presence of TiO2 P-25 as a photocatalyst, in addition to a decrease in the intensity of the maximum of the main absorption peak of MB located at 663 nm, an important blue shift of this band took place (from 663 nm to ∼636 nm). This blue shift can be associated with the demethylation process in the auxochrome groups of MB, which produces the demethylated dyes (Azure A, Azure B and Azure C) that upon subsequent oxidation yield the colorless products leucomethylene blue (the colorless compound of methylene blue, LMB) or LMB sulfoxides [39 and references therein]. This result explains the difference in the percentage of decolorization of the MB solution (77%) and mineralization of the initial TOC concentration (39%) obtained with TiO2 P-25 catalyst (Table 4). The lower degree of mineralization in this case can be attributed to the presence of some colorless reaction subproducts, resulting in an incomplete mineralization of MB. In the case of the TiO2/SBA-15, SnO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 catalysts, the principal change in the absorbance spectra of MB was a decrease in the intensity of the absorbance peak at 663 nm, while a blue shift of this band was less notorious (Fig. 9).

It was reported previously that the degradation mechanism of MB on TiO2-, SnO2- and ZnO-based photocatalysts is the standard photodegradation process [23,44–46]. This mechanism starts when the catalyst is irradiated with an energy greater than its Eg value, leading to the activation of the photocatalyst and the formation of electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs, able to migrate to the surface of the metal oxide. The holes are capable to generate hydroxyl radicals (OH•) through the oxidation of adsorbed water or hydroxyl anions, while the electrons lead to the formation of peroxy- and hydroperoxide radicals (O2 −•, HO2 •) upon the reduction of oxygen. Finally, the produced radicals participate in the degradation of MB through the production of different intermediates, achieving their complete mineralization. In addition, it was reported that the decrease in the concentration of MB over heterogeneous TiO2 catalysts is achieved by two processes: the adsorption of MB on the catalyst's surface and the photodegradation of the dye [15]. Lazar et al. [38] observed a preferential adsorption of MB when titania showed negative surface charge. It was also reported that the photodegradation of MB under UV irradiation was improved when titania was dispersed on the SBA-15 surface due to an increase in the adsorption capability of TiO2/SBA-15 composites as compared with that of the commercial P25 [15,21,47]. A similar effect was also observed in the photodegradation of the alizarin dye [19]. Mihai et al. [23] observed that the ZnO/SBA-15 catalyst showed a better catalytic performance in MB degradation than the ZnO/MCM-41 catalyst, also due to a better dispersion of zinc oxide on the SBA-15 surface. Resuming the above, the enhancement in the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 systems is generally attributed to two reasons: 1) the better dispersion of TiO2 or ZnO crystallites on the SBA-15 surface, increasing the specific surface area of these semiconductors and consequently the number of active sites available for the interaction with MB molecules, and 2) an increase in the dye adsorption capacity of the photocatalysts. Thereby, the high adsorption capacity, the high specific surface area and the small crystallite size play a fundamental role in the successful photodegradation of the MB dye. On the other hand, the lower photocatalytic activity of the SnO2/SBA-15 catalyst in MB photodegradation can be due to the poor dispersion of tin dioxide on the SBA-15 surface, as detected by TEM and XRD characterizations, which evidenced the agglomeration of SnO2 on the external surface of the support with the formation of large (∼20–70 nm) SnO2 particles. Despite of this, the SnO2/SBA-15 catalyst showed higher mineralization of MB (Table 4) than the commercial TiO2 P25 catalyst. Previously, it was observed that when SnO2 was well dispersed inside the SBA-15 pores, the photocatalytic activity was improved [27,28]. The above results illustrate the importance of the dispersion of the semiconductor metal oxides on the SBA-15 surface for their photocatalytic performance and the necessity to use the appropriate preparation method to reach a good dispersion and improve the photodegradation of MB.

In the present work, TiO2-, ZnO- and SnO2-containing SBA-15 catalysts were synthesized by the incipient wetness impregnation method, characterized and evaluated in the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue. The TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 catalysts showed significantly higher photocatalytic activity, in both sets of experiments (Ads + UV or direct UV irradiation), than the reference titania Degussa P25 or unsupported TiO2, ZnO and SnO2 nanopowders. For all SBA-15-supported catalysts, the methylene blue dye was removed from aqueous solutions by both adsorption and photocatalytic degradation. The ZnO/SBA-15 sample showed the highest MB adsorption among all synthesized catalysts. In this case, MB adsorption took place through two parallel mechanisms: the electrostatic interaction of MB+ cations with the negatively-charged catalyst's surface and the chemical precipitation of MB due to the interaction with basic OH− groups of the zinc oxide. In the TiO2/SBA-15 and SnO2/SBA-15 catalysts, the deposited metal oxides were more agglomerated than in the ZnO/SBA-15 one. Large agglomerates of SnO2 crystallites were observed on the external surface of the SnO2/SBA-15 sample by TEM, leading to lower activity in photocatalytic degradation and mineralization of MB than with the TiO2/SBA-15 and ZnO/SBA-15 catalysts. Despite of this, all the catalysts supported on SBA-15 showed a higher percentage of MB mineralization than the reference TiO2–P25 sample. Finally, it was demonstrated that the deposition of semiconductor metal oxides active in the photocatalytic degradation of cationic dyes on the SBA-15 support not only increases their photocatalytic activity, but also improves the dye removal by adsorption, and facilitates the separation of the catalysts from the reaction solution after the catalytic tests, allowing their possible reuse. In addition, deposition of ZnO on the SBA-15 surface prevented the photocorrosion of ZnO that is an important advantage compared to pure ZnO nanoparticles.
